task: Parse an AWS CloudFormation template in JSON format and extract all resource logical IDs
solution_criteria:
- The function must accept a single string parameter annotated with type hints (e.g., `template_str: str`) and return a `list[str]` of logical IDs
- The function must raise a `TypeError` with a descriptive message if the input is `None` or any non-string type (both cases are covered by a single `isinstance(template_str, str)` check)
- The function must catch `json.JSONDecodeError` when parsing malformed JSON and raise a `ValueError` with a descriptive message
- The function must validate that the result of `json.loads` is a `dict`; if it is any other type (e.g., a list, scalar, or `None`), raise a `ValueError` with a descriptive message before attempting any key access
- The function must validate that the parsed dict contains a top-level `Resources` key whose value is itself a `dict`; raise a `ValueError` with a descriptive message if the key is absent or if its value is not a `dict`
- The function must return a list of all keys from the `Resources` dictionary as the logical IDs; if `Resources` is a valid but empty `dict`, return an empty list — do NOT raise a `ValueError` in this case
- The function must include a docstring describing: the expected input format (a JSON-encoded CloudFormation template string), the return value (list of logical ID strings), and all exceptions that may be raised (`TypeError`, `ValueError`) with the conditions that trigger each

task: Write a Python function to validate AWS S3 bucket names according to AWS naming rules
solution_criteria:
The function must accept a string and return True only if the name satisfies ALL of the following AWS S3 bucket naming rules, otherwise return False:
- Length must be between 3 and 63 characters (inclusive); enforce this with exactly ONE authoritative mechanism — either embed `{3,63}` directly in the regex pattern OR use an explicit `len()` check, but never both; any solution that checks length in two places (e.g., a regex quantifier AND a separate `len()` guard) is invalid
- The regex pattern, if used, must include `{3,63}` length bounds directly in the quantifier so that no separate `len()` check is needed; if a `len()` check is used instead, the regex must not also constrain length
- Only lowercase letters (a-z), digits (0-9), and hyphens (-) are allowed; dots (.) are not permitted in this implementation
- Must start and end with a lowercase letter or digit (not a hyphen)
- Must not contain consecutive hyphens (e.g., 'my--bucket' is invalid)
- Must not be formatted as an IPv4 address (i.e., reject strings matching the pattern of four dot-separated octets such as '192.168.1.1'); since dots are already excluded by the character rule, explicitly document this rejection in the docstring
- Every variable defined in the function must be used; defining a regex pattern variable that is never referenced in the logic is not permitted
- The function must include a docstring that states each rule being enforced and clarifies that the function returns False (not raises an exception) for any violation
- Do not include redundant validation logic; if a single regex enforces all constraints (character set, length, start/end characters, consecutive hyphens), no additional conditional checks for those same constraints are needed

task: Create a regex pattern to match valid AWS IAM role ARNs
solution_criteria:
- The regex must match all three AWS partitions: `aws`, `aws-us-gov`, and `aws-cn`, e.g. `arn:aws:iam::`, `arn:aws-us-gov:iam::`, and `arn:aws-cn:iam::`
- The account ID capture group must match exactly 12 digits
- The role name capture group must match valid IAM role name characters (alphanumeric, `+`, `=`, `,`, `.`, `@`, `_`, `-`) with length 1–64
- The `match_arn` function must guard against `None` and empty string inputs using an explicit check `if arn is None or arn == ''` (not `if not arn`) and return `None` in both cases without raising an exception
- Include an assertion confirming that a role name of exactly 64 valid characters matches, and a separate assertion confirming that a role name of 65 characters does NOT match, directly testing the upper bound of the length constraint
- Include an assertion with a role name consisting solely of all valid special characters (`+=,.@_-`) to confirm the full character class is accepted
- Include at least 3 valid ARN examples (covering different partitions) and 3 invalid ARN examples (e.g. wrong format, non-12-digit account, missing role) with assertions confirming correct match or non-match behavior and that capture groups return the expected account ID and role name values

task: Write a Python function to convert AWS EC2 instance metadata JSON response into a flattened dictionary with dot-notation keys
solution_criteria:
- Accept input as either a pre-parsed dict or a raw JSON string; call json.loads() explicitly on string input before any further processing
- Raise a ValueError with a descriptive message if input is None or not a dict or str type, checked before any parsing or flattening
- Raise a ValueError with a descriptive message if a JSON string cannot be parsed by json.loads()
- If the root value after parsing is not a dict, return a flat dictionary with a single empty-string key mapped to the scalar value, making this edge case explicit and intentional
- Return a flat dictionary where all nested keys are joined with dot-notation (e.g., 'network.interfaces.0.mac'); list indices must be converted to strings explicitly using str(i) before concatenation, not implicitly via f-string formatting
- Enforce a recursion depth limit using a depth counter that raises a ValueError when depth >= max_depth (not strictly greater than), so that recursion never exceeds max_depth levels; use a default max_depth of 20
- Include a complete docstring describing parameters, return type, all raised exceptions, the dot-notation key format, and at least one example using realistic EC2 metadata
- Include type hints for all parameters and the return value using standard Python typing conventions

task: Create a regex pattern to extract AWS access key IDs from logs or configuration files
solution_criteria:
- The regex must use a capture group (parentheses) that wraps the entire key pattern so the full key is returned via match group(1) or equivalent
- The pattern must match all common AWS key ID prefixes: AKIA, ASIA, AROA, and AIDA (not just AKIA)
- After the 4-character prefix, the pattern must match exactly 16 uppercase alphanumeric characters (A-Z, 0-9), for a total key length of 20 characters
- The pattern must include word boundary assertions (\b) or equivalent non-word-character anchors on both sides of the full key to prevent false positives from partial matches within longer strings (e.g., 'XAKIA...' must not match); the trailing \b is required even though [A-Z0-9]{16} ends on a word character, to make the boundary intent explicit and documented
- The regex must be compiled or invoked with re.ASCII (re.A) flag explicitly set to enforce ASCII-only matching and make case-sensitivity unambiguous, rather than relying on Python's default behavior
- The solution must include a brief inline comment or docstring explaining that re.ASCII is used to enforce case-sensitive, ASCII-only matching and prevent Unicode word characters from affecting boundary detection
- The solution must not use re.IGNORECASE under any circumstances
- The function must accept a string input and return a list of all matched key IDs found in that string, supporting log parsing scenarios with multiple keys per input

task: Write a Python function to parse AWS CloudWatch log timestamps and convert them to a human-readable format
solution_criteria:
- Accept a single parameter typed as `int` with a descriptive name (e.g., `timestamp_ms`), and include a complete docstring describing the parameter, return value, any exceptions raised, and at least one runnable `doctest` example demonstrating correct usage
- Explicitly reject `bool` values before the `int` check by raising `TypeError` with a descriptive message (e.g., `'timestamp_ms must be an int, not bool'`), since `bool` is a subclass of `int` and would otherwise pass `isinstance` validation silently
- Validate the input: raise `TypeError` if the value is not an `int` (and not a `bool`), and raise `ValueError` if the value is negative or represents a timestamp beyond a reasonable upper bound
- Define the upper bound constant (e.g., `MAX_MS = 253402300799999`) with an inline comment explicitly stating its derivation (e.g., `# maximum milliseconds for year 9999-12-31 23:59:59 UTC`) to aid maintainability
- Handle `None` explicitly by raising `TypeError` with a descriptive message before any other validation or conversion logic
- Convert the milliseconds timestamp to seconds by dividing by 1000 before constructing a UTC-aware `datetime` object using `datetime.fromtimestamp` with `timezone.utc` or `datetime.utcfromtimestamp`
- Format the output using `strftime` with an explicit format string (e.g., `"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"`) to produce a consistent ISO 8601 string without microseconds or offset suffixes
- Include type hints for both the parameter (`int`) and the return value (`str`)

task: Create a JSON schema that validates the structure of an AWS Lambda function configuration object
solution_criteria:
The JSON schema should define required fields (FunctionName, Runtime, Handler, Role) with correct data types, and must satisfy the following precise requirements:
- FunctionName must match pattern ^[a-zA-Z0-9-_:]+$ and have maxLength of 140 to support both plain names (up to 64 chars) and full ARN format (up to 140 chars)
- Runtime must be an enum restricted to valid AWS Lambda runtime strings (e.g., python3.11, nodejs18.x, java17, etc.)
- Handler must include a pattern constraint (e.g., ^[^\s]+$ or similar) and a maxLength of 128 to reflect AWS limits
- Role must match an ARN pattern such as ^arn:aws:iam::[0-9]{12}:role/.+$
- VpcConfig, when present, must require SubnetIds and SecurityGroupIds as arrays each with minItems of 1
- EphemeralStorage must be supported as an optional field with a nested Size property (integer, minimum 512, maximum 10240)
- Environment Variables keys must be strictly enforced using a propertyNames constraint with pattern ^[a-zA-Z][a-zA-Z0-9_]*$ so that ALL keys in the Variables object are validated — relying solely on patternProperties without propertyNames or additionalProperties:false is insufficient and will not be accepted
- The Environment Variables Variables object must set additionalProperties:false alongside patternProperties, OR use propertyNames, to prevent keys that do not match the naming pattern from being accepted
- The Environment Variables object must enforce a maximum of 128 key-value pairs via maxProperties to reflect AWS limits
- Timeout must be an integer with minimum 1 and maximum 900
- MemorySize must be an integer with minimum 128 and maximum 10240
- The schema must declare \"$schema\": \"http://json-schema.org/draft-07/schema#\" and must not use draft-2019-09 or later keywords such as unevaluatedProperties that are unsupported in draft-07

task: Write a Python function to filter AWS DynamoDB table items based on a simple attribute condition
solution_criteria:
- The function must accept a list of item dictionaries in DynamoDB boto3 typed format (e.g., `{'attr': {'S': 'value'}}` or `{'attr': {'N': '123'}}`) and a condition specified as an attribute name and a plain Python value, returning only items where the typed attribute value matches the condition value after extracting the inner typed value
- The function must support at minimum the DynamoDB types `S` (string), `N` (numeric string compared as number), and `BOOL` (boolean)
- For `N` type comparisons, both the stored value (a numeric string) and the condition value must be converted to `float` before comparison, ensuring values like `'1'` and `1.0` are treated as equal
- The function must validate that `attribute_name` is a non-empty string and raise a `ValueError` if it is `None`, not a string, or an empty string
- The function must validate inputs and raise a `ValueError` if the items list is `None` or contains non-dict elements, and silently skip items that do not contain the specified attribute
- When encountering an unsupported DynamoDB type key during extraction, the function must raise a `ValueError` explicitly identifying the unsupported type, rather than returning `None` or silently skipping
- The function must include full type hints on all parameters and the return value
- The function must include a docstring describing parameters, return value, and raised exceptions including the new `ValueError` cases for invalid `attribute_name` and unsupported DynamoDB types

task: Create a regex pattern to match and extract AWS region codes from various AWS service endpoints
solution_criteria:
- The regex must match standard AWS region codes (e.g., us-east-1, eu-west-2, ap-southeast-1) as well as AWS GovCloud regions in three-segment format (e.g., us-gov-west-1, us-gov-east-1), using a flexible pattern that does not hardcode exact character counts for any segment.
- The pattern must capture the region code from all common AWS endpoint URL formats: standard service endpoints (service.region.amazonaws.com), S3 path-style URLs (s3.region.amazonaws.com/bucket), S3 virtual-hosted-style URLs (bucket.s3.region.amazonaws.com), and endpoints with additional subdomains (sub.service.region.amazonaws.com).
- The region must be captured in a named capture group (e.g., (?P<region>...)) so it can be extracted directly without relying on fixed positional groups or string splitting.
- The region pattern must account for the full known structure of AWS region codes: one or more lowercase letter segments for the geographic area and direction, followed by a single numeric identifier segment (e.g., -1, -2), without hardcoding exact character counts.
- The regex must NOT use variable-width lookbehinds (e.g., (?<=[.\-]) with alternatives); it must be fully compatible with Python's standard re module (re.search or re.findall) without requiring the third-party regex module — any lookbehind used must be strictly fixed-width.
- Instead of relying on lookbehinds to detect the token boundary before the region, the pattern must use a forward-anchored structural approach: explicitly match the known token immediately preceding the region (e.g., a literal dot followed by a known service token or a dot-separated segment) so the region is identified by its position relative to .amazonaws.com.
- The pattern must anchor the region match relative to the .amazonaws.com suffix — specifically, the named region group must appear in the segment(s) directly preceding the literal string .amazonaws.com — so that tokens like 's3', 'amazonaws', or bucket names are never captured as the region.
- The regex must not produce false positives by matching non-region dot-separated tokens such as 's3' or 'amazonaws' as the region value.
- The solution must be a single regular expression string, usable directly with Python's re module, and must correctly extract only the region portion from a provided test URL string.

task: Write a Python function to calculate AWS S3 storage costs based on object size and storage class
solution_criteria:
- Accept `size_bytes` (int/float) and `storage_class` (str) parameters; raise `ValueError` for negative `size_bytes` or unrecognized `storage_class` values
- Support storage classes: `STANDARD`, `STANDARD_IA`, `ONEZONE_IA`, `INTELLIGENT_TIERING`, `GLACIER`, `GLACIER_DEEP_ARCHIVE`, `GLACIER_INSTANT_RETRIEVAL`
- Apply AWS tiered pricing for `STANDARD`: $0.023/GB for first 50TB, $0.022/GB for next 450TB, $0.021/GB beyond 500TB
- For `STANDARD_IA` and `ONEZONE_IA`, enforce a minimum billable object size of 128KB AND a minimum storage duration of 30 days: compute cost as `max(size_bytes, 128KB) * rate_per_GB / GB * (30/30)`, ensuring the 30-day minimum duration is always applied regardless of actual storage duration input
- For `GLACIER_INSTANT_RETRIEVAL`, enforce a minimum billable object size of 128KB AND a minimum storage duration of 90 days: prorate the minimum duration charge into a single monthly cost by computing `max(size_bytes, 128KB) * rate_per_GB / GB * (90/30) / (90/30)` — i.e., bill for the full 90-day minimum but express it as a monthly equivalent by dividing total minimum-duration cost by 3
- For `GLACIER`, enforce a minimum billable size of `max(size_bytes, 40KB) + 8KB` overhead AND a 90-day minimum duration: compute monthly cost as `billable_size * rate_per_GB / GB * (90/30) / 3` — multiply by the minimum duration in months then divide by that same number so the result is a single prorated monthly charge, NOT the total cost over the full duration
- For `GLACIER_DEEP_ARCHIVE`, enforce a minimum billable size of `max(size_bytes, 40KB)` AND a 180-day minimum duration: compute monthly cost as `billable_size * rate_per_GB / GB * (180/30) / 6` — same proration logic as GLACIER, yielding one month's equivalent charge
- For `INTELLIGENT_TIERING`, assume 100% frequent access tier by default and document this assumption in a code comment; apply the frequent access rate ($0.023/GB) to the full object size; add a per-object monitoring fee of `$0.0025 / 1000` (i.e., $0.0000025 per object per month) — do NOT multiply this fee by 1000 or treat it as a flat $0.0025 charge
- Include a clearly commented pricing constants block at the top of the function with a note indicating the date these values were last verified against AWS public pricing
- Return the total estimated monthly cost in USD as a float rounded to 10 decimal places
